To create a plant UML diagram based on the given description:

1. Create a class that represents a single plant (e.g., a tomato, almond).
2. Define the `__init__(self)` method to accept the `plant` object and initialize it with its properties like `color`, `shape`, `fruit`, `density`.
3. Add an `attr_to_value(value, self)` method to handle the value of the `attribute` that you want to store in the diagram, e.g., `color="red"` or `density=100`.
4. Define a `__dict__` attribute to store the dictionary of plant properties like `name="tomato"`, `shape='oval'`, and so on.
5. Add an `__eq__(self, other)` method to check if two plants are equal in the sense that they have the same attributes or property values.
6. Define a `__eq__(self, other)` method to compare the properties of two plants with respect to their `type` attribute.
7. Create a string representation of the diagram using PlantUML's built-in string formatting capabilities (e.g., `"Tomato:color:red;shape:oval;density:100;"`).
8. Apply appropriate formatting techniques, such as `'+'`, `'-='`, and `<>` to separate the parts of the diagram into individual elements or nodes.
9. Add an `__eq__(self)` method to compare the properties of two other plants with respect to their `type` attribute if applicable (e.g., "Tomato:color:red;shape:oval;density:100" == "Tomato:color:red;").
10. Define additional features, such as `__repr__(self)` or `__eq__(other, other)` methods for the class to extend the diagram with more features (e.g., `'tomato2': tomato;`).

Here is an example of a plant UML diagram that meets these requirements:
```html
<!-- PlantUML diagrams -->
<root>
  <label>Tomato:color:red</label>
  <attr_to_value(color="red", self)>
    <node text="Red" type="oval">
        <property value="shape">
            <node>
                <type>area</type>
                    <node>
                        <type>volume</type>
                        <node>
                            <value>
                                      </value>
                        </node>
                        <node>
                            <type>count</type>
                                <node>
                                    <value>
                                      </value>
                        </node>
                    </property>
                </node>
            </role>
        </role>
      </label>
    </attr_to_value>
  </attribute>
</root>
```

To create a plant UML diagram based on the given description:

1. Create a class that represents a single plant (e.g., a tomato).
2. Define the `__init__(self)` method to accept the `plant` object and initialize it with its properties like `color`, `shape`, `fruit`, `density`.
3. Add an `attr_to_value(value, self)` method to handle the value of the `attribute` that you want to store in the diagram, e.g., `color="red"`.
4. Define an `__dict__` attribute to store the dictionary of plant properties like `name="tomato"`, `shape='oval'`, and so on.
5. Add an `__eq__(self, other)` method to check if two plants are equal in the sense that they have the same attributes or property values.
6. Define a `__eq__(self, other)` method to compare the properties of two others with respect to their `type` attribute.
7. Create a string representation of the diagram using PlantUML's built-in string formatting capabilities (e.g., `"Tomato:color:red;shape:oval;"`).
8. Apply appropriate formatting techniques, such as `'+'`, `'-='`, and `<>` to separate the parts of the diagram into individual elements or nodes.
9. Add an `__eq__(self, other)` method to compare the properties of two other plants with respect to their `type` attribute if applicable (e.g., "Tomato:color:red;shape:" == "Tomato:color:red;").
10. Define additional features, such as `__repr__(self, other, other)`, which allow you to return only valid PlantUML code wrapped in @startuml and @enduml tags or points.
9. Add an `__eq__()` method to compare the properties of two others with respect to their `type` attribute if applicable (e.g., "Tomato:color:" == "Tomato:color;").
10. Define additional features, such as `__repr__()`, which allow you to return only valid PlantUML code wrapped in @startuml and @enduml tags or points or points.
